reasoning:
The question revolves around whether the Wikipedia article "List of infectious diseases" will list a vaccine for Rocky Mountain spotted fever (RMSF) by the four resolution dates in 2025. As of May 2025, the article indicates "No" (typically an empty cell in the "Vaccine(s)" column), and authoritative sources like the CDC explicitly state there is currently no vaccine to prevent RMSF. 

There was a historical vaccine (the Spencer-Parker vaccine) developed in the 1920s, but it was rendered obsolete by the discovery of antibiotics in the 1940s. Current research in 2024 and 2025 regarding a modern RMSF vaccine involves early-stage preclinical work (e.g., using computational design and identifying therapeutic targets), meaning no new vaccine is anywhere close to clinical trials or regulatory approval. 

For the Wikipedia page to resolve to "Yes", an editor would either have to mistakenly add an experimental vaccine as if it were approved, or add the obsolete Spencer-Parker vaccine and have it remain on the page on the resolution date. Given the CDC's unequivocal stance that no vaccine exists, any such edit is unlikely and would quickly be reverted. Therefore, the probability remains at the absolute floor of 2% for the immediate dates, rising only marginally to 4% for the furthest date to account for the infinitesimally small chance of a random Wikipedia edit persisting or an unforeseen historical update.
